http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2017/03/02/sports/05WARRIORS1/05WARRIORS1-jumbo.jpg?quality=75&amp;auto=webp 1024w, http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2017/03/02/sports/05WARRIORS1/05WARRIORS1-superJumbo.jpg?quality=75&amp;auto=webp 2048w" sizes="((min-width: 600px) and (max-width: 1004px)) 84vw, (min-width: 1005px) 80vw, 100vw" decoding="async" width="600" height="400"/></div><figcaption class="css-1ifeaca e1maroi60"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Roberto Gallinat of South Plains College delivered a dunk in a game against New Mexico Junior College last month in Levelland, Tex.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div><div data-testid="byline-timestamp" class="css-xt80pu eakwutd0"><div class="css-103l8m3"><div class="css-1e2jphy epjyd6m1"><div class="css-233int epjyd6m0"><p class="css-4anu6l e1jsehar1"><span class="byline-prefix">By </span><span class="css-1baulvz last-byline" itemprop="name">Scott Cacciola</span></p></div></div><div data-testid="reading-time-module" class="css-8atqhb"><ul class="css-1cgskve epjyd6m3"><li class="css-ccw2r3 epjyd6m2"><time class="css-1z1nqv e16638kd0" datetime="2017-03-02T05:00:45-05:00">March 2, 2017</time></li></ul></div></div></div></header><section name="articleBody" class="meteredContent css-1r7ky0e"><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">LEVELLAND, Tex. — Steve Green, the men’s basketball coach at South Plains College, was recovering from an up-and-down season last summer when he decided he needed to make a change. So he planted himself in front of the television in his living room and began to study — really study — his favorite team.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Green scribbled down every cut, every back screen and every curl. In pursuit of a goal that was so ambitious that it bordered on audacious, he consulted with his assistants and overhauled his playbook. His wife, Teresa, discovered that she needed to make room for a new love in her husband’s life.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“He is obsessed,” she said, “with the Warriors.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">In his grand experiment to turn his team into the junior college version of the Golden State Warriors — yes, <em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">those</em> Warriors — Green has coached the South Plains Texans to a 28-0 record as they enter the postseason.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-bkbwf1 e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Steve Green, coach of the South Plains College Texans, instructing his players during a practice. Green, whose favorite team is the Golden State Warriors, models his plays on those of the N.B.A. team.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:258.4561403508772px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">South Plains players performed a routine during introductions before playing New Mexico Junior College.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">South Plains, the top-ranked junior college team in the country, averages 98.4 points a game, shooting 52.2 percent from the field. They spread the floor, move the ball and shoot jumpers from Siberia, averaging 11.7 3-pointers a game.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">If that all sounds familiar, it should: The Texans run the Warriors’ plays.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Green, 63, has never met Steve Kerr, the coach of the Warriors, nor has he had the chance to watch the N.B.A.’s highest-octane team play in person. But the Warriors’ growing influence is apparent here in Levelland, a small city of 14,000 set amid cotton fields and oil rigs about 30 miles west of Lubbock.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">South Plains plays its home games at the Texan Dome, a 3,300-seat arena that features championship rodeo banners and sometimes leaks in rainy weather. But before his team fires off one 3-pointer after another and dunks opponents to smithereens, Green meets with his assistants in a windowless conference room where, among more prosaic items of business, they watch the Warriors, discuss the Warriors and plagiarize the Warriors.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“We’re going to put this new one in today,” Green said on a recent morning as he fiddled with a short video clip on his iPhone. “They ran this against Memphis the other night, and I hadn’t seen them do this before.”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">In the fourth quarter of a victory against the Grizzlies last month, the Warriors unveiled a play that excited Green. Shaun Livingston, the Warriors’ backup point guard, threw a pass to James Michael McAdoo at the right elbow. Over at the left elbow, Kevin Durant set a screen for Klay Thompson, who then took a handoff from McAdoo. As two defenders converged on him, Thompson slipped a bounce pass to a cutting McAdoo for a dunk.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">The basket put the Warriors up by 24. In other words, the sequence was both forgettable and inconsequential — to everyone, perhaps, except Green, and only because he watches every second of every Warriors game.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Josh Webster of the South Plains Texans with the ball. In searching for driving lanes, Webster tries to emulate Shaun Livingston, a Warriors guard.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Green has no idea what the Warriors actually call their plays — they keep them secret — so he just makes up his own names as he goes. The one involving McAdoo and Thompson would, in South Plains-ese, be “Horns 52.” Green described his creative process.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“We’ll put something new in, and the guys will be like, ‘What are you going to call it?’” he said. “And I’ll say: ‘I don’t know. What do you guys want to call it?’”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">It should be noted that not everything South Plains does comes straight from the Warriors’ playbook. The Texans run weaves in transition, and Green is fond of some principles of the motion offense.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Most of the time, though, he is channeling Golden State. That means fewer pick-and-rolls in favor of greater movement away from the ball, including flare screens and back screens designed to spring shooters free from defenders. The ball zips from teammate to teammate.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“For a long time in the N.B.A., people got away from the five-man game,” Kerr said in a telephone interview on Wednesday as he discussed the impact his team was having on college programs. “It was more of an isolation game. But people are going smaller and faster. And for us, I think the key is that we don’t hold the ball. We almost always have five playmakers on the floor. The ball moves, and nobody dominates it.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">In terms of getting his team to function like the Warriors, Green has found that it helps to have good players. (Kerr concurred.) Jordan Brangers, the starting shooting guard at South Plains, averages 21.8 points a game and shoots 48.7 percent from the 3-point line. In a lopsided victory last month, Brangers, who is 6 feet 2 inches, shot 14 of 19 from the field and sank 10 of 15 3-pointers.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">His coach sees shades of the Warriors’ Stephen Curry. “The kid does not miss an open shot,” Green said.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Texans guard Jordan Brangers shot a deep 3-pointer over New Mexico Junior College’s Xabier Gomez.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">South Plains players Shawntrez Davis (15), Justice Green (12) and Ibrahima Diagne (20) celebrated a 3-point shot in the game against New Mexico Junior College on Feb. 13.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Like many of his teammates, <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/http://www.spctexans.com/sports/mbkb/2016-17/bios/brangers_jordan_dpym" title="SPC link" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Brangers traveled a long road to South Plains</a>. He was a standout high school player in Radcliff, Ky., before dropping out in his senior year because of what he described as personal and academic issues.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“I thought I was done with organized basketball,” Brangers said.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">After a coach persuaded him to earn his G.E.D., he briefly attended two small colleges without playing a single minute of basketball, he said. He returned home to Kentucky, filling his days with pickup hoops.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Then, one afternoon, Green showed up. He had heard about Brangers from a friend. Green watched him shoot for about two minutes.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“Are you kidding me?” Green recalled saying.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">At South Plains, Brangers has thrived. Green often sends him late-night texts that include 10-second video bursts of Curry or Thompson performing various feats of basketball wizardry. Green is not known for his technical savvy. He records the clips off his television using his iPhone, then hits “send.” The clips can be fuzzy — more celluloid than high def — but he gets his point across: <em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">See how Thompson got open here?</em></p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“It’s really just understanding how those guys come off screens,” Brangers said.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Jahlil Tripp, a forward from Brooklyn playing for South Plains College, with Damon Stoudamire, the University of the Pacific head coach.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">If Brangers operates as a Curry-Thompson hybrid, Steve Green has shaped <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/http://www.spctexans.com/sports/mbkb/2016-17/bios/tripp_jahill_iwpn" title="SPC link" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Jahlil Tripp, his starting power forward</a>, into a facsimile of the Warriors’ Draymond Green: a versatile player who can handle the ball, shoot with range and defend multiple positions. Tripp, a product of Lincoln High in Brooklyn, averages 11.4 points and 6.6 rebounds while playing just 17.3 minutes a game. The Texans spread the wealth: Ten players average at least 10 minutes a game.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">He said he would prefer to be known as a college version of the Cavaliers’ LeBron James, his favorite player.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“But Coach wants me to be Draymond,” said Tripp, who has scholarship offers from schools like Hofstra, Indiana State and the University of the Pacific. “So I’m Draymond.”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">And there is <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/http://www.scout.com/player/213236-josh-webster" title="Scout link" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Josh Webster, the starting point guard</a>, who tries to emulate Livingston, a lanky and efficient playmaker. Like Livingston, Webster is perpetually searching for driving lanes as teammates drag defenders to the 3-point line. Webster, who is bound for Texas Tech with Brangers next season, shoots 62.6 percent from the field, which goes to the core of Green’s revamped approach.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“I just want guys who can shoot now,” he said. “If you have somebody on the floor who can’t score, you’re playing four against five. They just don’t guard them.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Green, in his 17th season at South Plains, is a decorated coach. He guided his team to national championships in 2008 and 2012, and it was the runner-up in 2015. But last season was a relative struggle. South Plains finished 21-9 and out of the playoff picture. Something was amiss.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Green had already been thinking about how the game was evolving. As a young coach, he said, he was schooled on the importance of defense. In fact, his first college job was as a graduate assistant to <a class="css-yywogo" href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/http://www.sports-reference.com/cbb/coaches/eddie-sutton-1.html" title="Web link" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">Eddie Sutton at Arkansas</a>, back when the Razorbacks were a grind-it-out, roll-up-your-sleeves squad of sturdy defenders.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">But in recent years, Green had seen how players were becoming more skilled on offense: power forwards who could put the ball on the floor and shoot 3-pointers, guards who could stretch defenders like rubber bands by pulling up from 25 feet. He had one such player, Marshall Henderson, who led South Plains to its national title in 2012.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Fans watched New Mexico Junior College shoot free throws during the game against South Plains.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Raquan Mitchell (3) of the South Plains Texans blocked a shot attempt by Kene Chukwuka (42) of New Mexico Junior College.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">Green recalled a conversation he had a few years ago with a colleague.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“Do you think we’ve gotten to a point now where you can’t really stop anybody anymore, but you just decide, hey, we’re going to allow you to take this shot instead of that shot?” Green recalled asking him. “I think we’ve all begun to realize — especially in the pros — that nobody can stop anybody one on one. I just think it’s more about the offensive end now than it is about the defensive end.”</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">At South Plains, Green wants his players to be strategic on defense: Suffocate opponents along the 3-point line and make life difficult for them in the paint. He is more than willing to cede midrange jumpers, the least efficient shot in basketball. The Texans have limited opponents to 28.3 percent shooting from 3-point range and outscored them by an average of 35.4 points a game.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">“They’re the best team in the country,” Brian Lohrey, the coach at New Mexico Junior College, said after watching South Plains drain 14 of 25 3-pointers in a 26-point win against his team last month. “There are a lot of good teams at this level. We’re good. But we are not good against them.”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">In so many ways, the players at South Plains could not be more removed from the glamour of the N.B.A. They ride a bus to all their road games, a mode of transport that Tripp assessed as a “real experience.” They live in a no-frills dorm across the street from the Texan Dome. They amuse themselves with post-practice games of one on one.</p><p class="css-at9mc1 evys1bk0">But they adhere to what Kerr described as the Warriors’ mentality: a willingness to pass and create for others. At South Plains, miles from nowhere, they do it as well as anyone.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-large css-hxpw2c e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><div class="css-nwd8t8" data-testid="lazy-image"><div data-testid="lazyimage-container" style="height:257.77777777777777px"></div></div></div><figcaption class="css-1g9ic6e ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-jevhma e13ogyst0">Banners hanging in the rafters of the Texan Dome in Levelland celebrated past championships.</span><span class="css-1u46b97 e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span><span aria-hidden="false">Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times</span></span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div></section><div class="bottom-of-article"><div class="css-ddxw8g"></div><div class="css-13ldwoe">A version of this article appears in print on <span class="css-1dmwf73"> </span>, Section <!-- -->SP<!-- -->, Page <!-- class="css-1vbto68 e1mu4ftr0"/><h2 id="styln-guide-title" class="css-pux3fz">Explore Our Sports Coverage</h2><h3 class="css-10thtcb">Dive deeper into the people, issues and trends shaping the sports world.</h3><hr class="css-1jihs6t e1mu4ftr0"/><ul><li class="css-1rcz39t"><p class="css-ttf587 evys1bk0"><strong>Collective Force of Head Hits: </strong>The largest study of chronic traumatic encephalopathy to date found that the <a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/06/20/sports/football/cte-study-concussions-brain-tackle.html?action=click&amp;pgtype=Article&amp;state=default&amp;module=styln-sports-general&amp;variant=show®ion=BELOW_MAIN_CONTENT&amp;block=storyline_flex_guide_recirc">cumulative force of head hits absorbed by players in their careers</a> is the best predictor of future brain disease.</p></li><li class="css-1rcz39t"><p class="css-ttf587 evys1bk0"><strong>‘I Can’t Continue This Fight’:</strong> Why Tyrell Terry <a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/06/19/sports/basketball/tyrell-terry-basketball-mental-health.html?action=click&amp;pgtype=Article&amp;state=default&amp;module=styln-sports-general&amp;variant=show®ion=BELOW_MAIN_CONTENT&amp;block=storyline_flex_guide_recirc">gave up basketball</a>, the millions of dollars that came with it and the identity he had spent his whole life building.</p></li><li class="css-1rcz39t"><p class="css-ttf587 evys1bk0"><strong>The Dirty Secrets of Groundskeepers:</strong> In an era of expertly manicured fields and shared knowledge among groundskeepers, crews have an unexpected tool in their arsenal: <a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/06/20/sports/baseball/mlb-groundskeepers-grass.html?action=click&amp;pgtype=Article&amp;state=default&amp;module=styln-sports-general&amp;variant=show®ion=BELOW_MAIN_CONTENT&amp;block=storyline_flex_guide_recirc">baseball’s ban on infield shifts</a>.</p></li><li class="css-1rcz39t"><p class="css-ttf587 evys1bk0"><strong>The Power of Walking Away:</strong> Napoli Coach Luciano Spalletti led his team to a title and then <a href="http://web.archive.org/web/20230623022857/https://www.nytimes.com/2023/06/16/sports/soccer/luciano-spalletti-jack-grealish.html?action=click&amp;pgtype=Article&amp;state=default&amp;module=styln-sports-general&amp;variant=show®ion=BELOW_MAIN_CONTENT&amp;block=storyline_flex_guide_recirc">left because the job was done</a>. That is worth celebrating.</p></li></ul></section></div><div id="bottom-sheet-sensor"><div></div></div><div><div id="bottom-wrapper" class="css-c4adj9"><div id="bottom-slug" Cacciola","__typename":"Byline"}],"role":[],"__typename":"BylineBlock"},"timestampBlock":{"timestamp":"2017-03-02T10:00:45.000Z","align":"DEFAULT","showUpdatedTimestamp":null,"__typename":"TimestampBlock"}},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"LEVELLAND, Tex. — Steve Green, the men’s basketball coach at South Plains College, was recovering from an up-and-down season last summer when he decided he needed to make a change. So he planted himself in front of the television in his living room and began to study — really study — his favorite team.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":0,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Green scribbled down every cut, every back screen and every curl. In pursuit of a goal that was so ambitious that it bordered on audacious, he consulted with his assistants and overhauled his playbook. His wife, Teresa, discovered that she needed to make room for a new love in her husband’s life.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":1,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“He is obsessed,” she said, “with the Warriors.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"In his grand experiment to turn his team into the junior college version of the Golden State Warriors — yes, ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"those","formats":[{"__typename":"ItalicFormat","type":null}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":" Warriors — Green has coached the South Plains Texans to a 28-0 record as they enter the postseason.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":2,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ImageBlock","size":"LARGE","media":{"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvZTIzYTkyMGEtZTQxZS01NTc3LWIxMjktZjhkMzU0YzVlMTVh","imageType":"photo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors41.html","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002Fe23a920a-e41e-5577-b129-f8d354c5e15a","credit":"Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times","legacyHtmlCaption":"Steve Green, coach of the South Plains College Texans, instructing his players during a practice. Green, whose favorite team is the Golden State Warriors, models his plays on those of the N.B.A. team.","crops":[{"renditions":[{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F03\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors41\u002F05warriors41-articleLarge.jpg","name":"articleLarge","width":600,"height":400,"__typename":"ImageRendition"},{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F03\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors41\u002F05warriors41-jumbo.jpg","name":"jumbo","width":1024,"height":683,"__typename":"ImageRendition"},{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F03\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors41\u002F05warriors41-popup.jpg","name":"popup","width":650,"height":433,"__typename":"ImageRendition"},{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F03\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors41\u002F05warriors41-superJumbo.jpg","name":"superJumbo","width":2048,"height":1365,"__typename":"ImageRendition"}],"__typename":"ImageCrop"},{"renditions":[{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F03\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors41\u002F05warriors41-articleInline.jpg","name":"articleInline","width":190,"height":127,"__typename":"ImageRendition"}],"__typename":"ImageCrop"}],"caption":{"text":"Steve Green, coach of the South Plains College Texans, instructing his players during a practice. Plains players performed a routine during introductions before playing New Mexico Junior College.","content":[{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"text":"South Plains players performed a routine during introductions before playing New Mexico Junior College.","__typename":"TextInline","formats":[]}]}],"__typename":"TextOnlyDocumentBlock"},"altText":"","__typename":"Image"}},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":4,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"South Plains, the top-ranked junior college team in the country, averages 98.4 points a game, shooting 52.2 percent from the field. They spread the floor, move the ball and shoot jumpers from Siberia, averaging 11.7 3-pointers a game.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":5,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"If that all sounds familiar, it should: The Texans run the Warriors’ plays.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Green, 63, has never met Steve Kerr, the coach of the Warriors, nor has he had the chance to watch the N.B.A.’s highest-octane team play in person. But the Warriors’ growing influence is apparent here in Levelland, a small city of 14,000 set amid cotton fields and oil rigs about 30 miles west of Lubbock.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":6,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"South Plains plays its home games at the Texan Dome, a 3,300-seat arena that features championship rodeo banners and sometimes leaks in rainy weather. But before his team fires off one 3-pointer after another and dunks opponents to smithereens, Green meets with his assistants in a windowless conference room where, among more prosaic items of business, they watch the Warriors, discuss the Warriors and plagiarize the Warriors.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":7,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“We’re going to put this new one in today,” Green said on a recent morning as he fiddled with a short video clip on his iPhone. “They ran this against Memphis the other night, and I hadn’t seen them do this before.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":true,"index":8,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"In the fourth quarter of a victory against the Grizzlies last month, the Warriors unveiled a play that excited Green. Shaun Livingston, the Warriors’ backup point guard, threw a pass to James Michael McAdoo at the right elbow. Over at the left elbow, Kevin Durant set a screen for Klay Thompson, who then took a handoff from McAdoo. As two defenders converged on him, Thompson slipped a bounce pass to a cutting McAdoo for a dunk.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":9,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"The basket put the Warriors up by 24. In other words, the sequence was both forgettable and inconsequential — to everyone, perhaps, except Green, and only because he watches every second of every Warriors game.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":10,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ImageBlock","size":"LARGE","media":{"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvY2UwYjk0ZDEtMjRjZC01ZjgxLWJkMWQtNThjZDRmYmZjMmYx","imageType":"photo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS4.html","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002Fce0b94d1-24cd-5f81-bd1d-58cd4fbfc2f1","credit":"Ivan Pierre Aguirre for The New York Times","legacyHtmlCaption":"Josh Webster of the South Plains Texans with the ball. In searching for driving lanes, Webster tries to emulate Shaun Livingston, a Warriors guard.","crops":[{"renditions":[{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS4\u002F05WARRIORS4-articleLarge.jpg","name":"articleLarge","width":600,"height":400,"__typename":"ImageRendition"},{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS4\u002F05WARRIORS4-jumbo.jpg","name":"jumbo","width":1024,"height":683,"__typename":"ImageRendition"},{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS4\u002F05WARRIORS4-popup.jpg","name":"popup","width":650,"height":433,"__typename":"ImageRendition"},{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS4\u002F05WARRIORS4-superJumbo.jpg","name":"superJumbo","width":2048,"height":1365,"__typename":"ImageRendition"}],"__typename":"ImageCrop"},{"renditions":[{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fstatic01.nyt.com\u002Fimages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS4\u002F05WARRIORS4-articleInline.jpg","name":"articleInline","width":190,"height":127,"__typename":"ImageRendition"}],"__typename":"ImageCrop"}],"caption":{"text":"Josh Webster of the South Plains Texans with the ball. In searching for driving lanes, Webster tries to emulate Shaun Livingston, a Warriors guard.","content":[{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","content":[{"text":"Josh Webster of the South Plains Texans with the ball. In searching for driving lanes, Webster tries to emulate Shaun Livingston, a Warriors guard.","__typename":"TextInline","formats":[]}]}],"__typename":"TextOnlyDocumentBlock"},"altText":"","__typename":"Image"}},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":11,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Green has no idea what the Warriors actually call their plays — they keep them secret — so he just makes up his own names as he goes. The one involving McAdoo and Thompson would, in South Plains-ese, be “Horns 52.” Green described his creative process.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":12,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“We’ll put something new in, and the guys will be like, ‘What are you going to call it?’” he said. “And I’ll say: ‘I don’t know. What do you guys want to call it?’”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":13,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"It should be noted that not everything South Plains does comes straight from the Warriors’ playbook. The Texans run weaves in transition, and Green is fond of some principles of the motion offense.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":14,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Most of the time, though, he is channeling Golden State. That means fewer pick-and-rolls in favor of greater movement away from the ball, including flare screens and back screens designed to spring shooters free from defenders. The ball zips from teammate to teammate.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":true,"index":15,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“For a long time in the N.B.A., people got away from the five-man game,” Kerr said in a telephone interview on Wednesday as he discussed the impact his team was having on college programs. “It was more of an isolation game. But people are going smaller and faster. And for us, I think the key is that we don’t hold the ball. We almost always have five playmakers on the floor. The ball moves, and nobody dominates it.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":16,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"In terms of getting his team to function like the Warriors, Green has found that it helps to have good players. (Kerr concurred.) Jordan Brangers, the starting shooting guard at South Plains, averages 21.8 points a game and shoots 48.7 percent from the 3-point line. In a lopsided victory last month, Brangers, who is 6 feet 2 inches, shot 14 of 19 from the field and sank 10 of 15 3-pointers.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":17,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"His He was a standout high school player in Radcliff, Ky., before dropping out in his senior year because of what he described as personal and academic issues.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":21,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“I thought I was done with organized basketball,” Brangers said.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"After a coach persuaded him to earn his G.E.D., he briefly attended two small colleges without playing a single minute of basketball, he said. He returned home to Kentucky, filling his days with pickup hoops.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":true,"index":22,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Then, one afternoon, Green showed up. He had heard about Brangers from a friend. Green watched him shoot for about two minutes.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":23,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“Are you kidding me?” Green recalled saying.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"At South Plains, Brangers has thrived. Green often sends him late-night texts that include 10-second video bursts of Curry or Thompson performing various feats of basketball wizardry. Green is not known for his technical savvy. He records the clips off his television using his iPhone, then hits “send.” The clips can be fuzzy — more celluloid than high def — but he gets his point across: ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"See how Thompson got open here?","formats":[{"__typename":"ItalicFormat","type":null}]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":24,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“It’s really just understanding how those guys come off screens,” Brangers said.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ImageBlock","size":"LARGE","media":{"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvNzZhYmU1OTEtZTBkMC01MjM4LWJkMWEtZTRmNTQ5NTgwODhl","imageType":"photo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS5.html","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F76abe591-e0d0-5238-bd1a-e4f54958088e","credit":"Ivan Brangers operates as a Curry-Thompson hybrid, Steve Green has shaped ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Jahlil Tripp, his starting power forward","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.spctexans.com\u002Fsports\u002Fmbkb\u002F2016-17\u002Fbios\u002Ftripp_jahill_iwpn","title":"SPC link"}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", into a facsimile of the Warriors’ Draymond Green: a versatile player who can handle the ball, shoot with range and defend multiple positions. Tripp, a product of Lincoln High in Brooklyn, averages 11.4 points and 6.6 rebounds while playing just 17.3 minutes a game. The Texans spread the wealth: Ten players average at least 10 minutes a game.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":26,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"He said he would prefer to be known as a college version of the Cavaliers’ LeBron James, his favorite player.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":27,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“But Coach wants me to be Draymond,” said Tripp, who has scholarship offers from schools like Hofstra, Indiana State and the University of the Pacific. “So I’m Draymond.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":28,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"And there is ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Josh Webster, the starting point guard","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.scout.com\u002Fplayer\u002F213236-josh-webster","title":"Scout link"}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", who tries to emulate Livingston, a lanky and efficient playmaker. Like Livingston, Webster is perpetually searching for driving lanes as teammates drag defenders to the 3-point line. Webster, who is bound for Texas Tech with Brangers next season, shoots 62.6 percent from the field, which goes to the core of Green’s revamped approach.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":true,"index":29,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“I just want guys who can shoot now,” he said. “If you have somebody on the floor who can’t score, you’re playing four against five. They just don’t guard them.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":30,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Green, in his 17th season at South Plains, is a decorated coach. He guided his team to national championships in 2008 and 2012, and it was the runner-up in 2015. But last season was a relative struggle. South Plains finished 21-9 and out of the playoff picture. Something was amiss.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":31,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Green had already been thinking about how the game was evolving. As a young coach, he said, he was schooled on the importance of defense. In fact, his first college job was as a graduate assistant to ","formats":[]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Eddie Sutton at Arkansas","formats":[{"__typename":"LinkFormat","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttp:\u002F\u002Fwww.sports-reference.com\u002Fcbb\u002Fcoaches\u002Feddie-sutton-1.html","title":"Web link"}]},{"__typename":"TextInline","text":", back when the Razorbacks were a grind-it-out, roll-up-your-sleeves squad of sturdy defenders.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":32,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"But in recent years, Green had seen how players were becoming more skilled on offense: power forwards who could put the ball on the floor and shoot 3-pointers, guards who could stretch defenders like rubber bands by pulling up from 25 feet. He had one such player, Marshall Henderson, who led South Plains to its national title in 2012.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":33,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ImageBlock","size":"LARGE","media":{"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvMThlZjY5MTAtYzBmNi01ZDUwLWJiMTQtNzc5NmJlYjU1OGI1","imageType":"photo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05warriors36.html","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F18ef6910-c0f6-5d50-bb14-7796beb558b5","credit":"Ivan College.","__typename":"TextInline","formats":[]}]}],"__typename":"TextOnlyDocumentBlock"},"altText":"","__typename":"Image"}},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":35,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"Green recalled a conversation he had a few years ago with a colleague.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“Do you think we’ve gotten to a point now where you can’t really stop anybody anymore, but you just decide, hey, we’re going to allow you to take this shot instead of that shot?” Green recalled asking him. “I think we’ve all begun to realize — especially in the pros — that nobody can stop anybody one on one. I just think it’s more about the offensive end now than it is about the defensive end.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":true,"index":36,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"At South Plains, Green wants his players to be strategic on defense: Suffocate opponents along the 3-point line and make life difficult for them in the paint. He is more than willing to cede midrange jumpers, the least efficient shot in basketball. The Texans have limited opponents to 28.3 percent shooting from 3-point range and outscored them by an average of 35.4 points a game.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":37,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"“They’re the best team in the country,” Brian Lohrey, the coach at New Mexico Junior College, said after watching South Plains drain 14 of 25 3-pointers in a 26-point win against his team last month. “There are a lot of good teams at this level. We’re good. But we are not good against them.”","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":true,"adsMobileHoldout":true,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":38,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"In so many ways, the players at South Plains could not be more removed from the glamour of the N.B.A. They ride a bus to all their road games, a mode of transport that Tripp assessed as a “real experience.” They live in a no-frills dorm across the street from the Texan Dome. They amuse themselves with post-practice games of one on one.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":true,"adsDesktop":false,"index":39,"bad":false},{"__typename":"ParagraphBlock","textAlign":"DEFAULT","content":[{"__typename":"TextInline","text":"But they adhere to what Kerr described as the Warriors’ mentality: a willingness to pass and create for others. At South Plains, miles from nowhere, they do it as well as anyone.","formats":[]}]},{"__typename":"Dropzone","adsDesktopHoldout":false,"adsMobileHoldout":false,"adsMobile":false,"adsDesktop":false,"index":40,"bad":true},{"__typename":"ImageBlock","size":"LARGE","media":{"id":"SW1hZ2U6bnl0Oi8vaW1hZ2UvMmQ2NDA2NWMtNWY1NC01ZjJjLWIzYmUtMTY2NzVkZjMzZWMy","imageType":"photo","url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20230623022857\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002Fimagepages\u002F2017\u002F03\u002F02\u002Fsports\u002F05WARRIORS7.html","uri":"nyt:\u002F\u002Fimage\u002F2d64065c-5f54-5f2c-b3be-16675df33ec2","credit":"Ivan
